clusters.primary_intent: What people usually mean by the 183-day tax residency rule, why it is common but not universal, and where day counting stops being the whole answer.

Arizona's residency rule in plain terms: spending more than nine months (about 270 days) of the tax year in Arizona presumes you are a resident, unless you rebut it. How to count it, and the official source.

Australia's 183-day test in plain terms: 183 or more days in an income year presumes you are a resident for tax, unless your usual place of abode is outside Australia. How to count it and the official ATO source.

California has no fixed day-count line for residency. It is a closest-connections test, but spending more than nine months (about 270 days) in California in a tax year creates a rebuttable presumption of residency. How the count works, and the official source.

Canada's 183-day rule in plain terms: sojourn 183 days or more in a calendar year and you are a deemed resident taxed on worldwide income. Plus the residential-ties test, how to count, and the official CRA source.

Colombia's 183-day tax residency rule in plain terms: more than 183 days in any rolling 365-day period makes you a tax resident. How to count it, what happens when days straddle two years, and the official source.

Colorado's six-month rule in plain terms: a permanent place of abode plus more than six months (more than 183 days) in Colorado in the tax year makes you a statutory resident. How to count it, and the official source.

Connecticut's 183-day rule in plain terms: a permanent place of abode plus more than 183 days in Connecticut in a taxable year makes you a statutory resident. How to count it, and the official source.
How major country-counting systems differ, what they are actually trying to count, and why travel totals stop being comparable when the list is unclear.
A practical overview of how common nomad destinations frame short stays, extensions, and longer remote-work routes, without pretending one summary can replace official programme rules.
How to think about layovers for immigration, day counting, and personal travel lists without pretending those questions all have the same answer.

Estonia's 183-day tax residency rule in plain terms: 183 days or more in any 12 consecutive calendar months makes you a tax resident. How to count it, plus the permanent-home test and the official source.
What an exported travel-history record is trying to achieve, when it helps, what makes it weak, and what to verify before relying on it for a visa application.
Flighty tracks your flights, not your days in each country. Here's how to turn your Flighty flight history into a visa and residency day count you can trust.

Georgia's (the country) 183-day tax residency rule in plain terms: 183 or more days in any continuous 12-month period ending in the tax year makes you a tax resident. How to count it, plus the official source.

Georgia the US state taxes you as a resident if you spend 183 days or more in Georgia during any 12 consecutive months, not just a calendar year. How the rolling day count works, and the official source.

Greece's 183-day tax residency rule in plain terms: more than 183 days in any 12-month period makes you a tax resident from your first day. How to count it, the centre of vital interests test, and the official source.

Hawaii's 200-day rule in plain terms: spending more than 200 days in Hawaii in the tax year creates a rebuttable presumption of residency. How the presumption works, how to rebut it, and the official source.
Why 195 is the usual answer, why travelers still encounter other numbers, and where a simple country count stops being a simple question.
What tracking travel days is actually trying to achieve, why spreadsheets and memory fail faster than people expect, and what makes a travel record usable over time.

Idaho's 270-day rule in plain terms: keep a home in Idaho and spend more than 270 days there in the tax year and you are a resident, even if domiciled elsewhere. How to count it, and the official source.
Ireland's tax residency rule in plain terms: 183 days or more in a tax year makes you resident, and a 280-day two-year test can too. How to count it and the official source.
What people usually mean by Japan's 90-day rule, how Japan differs from Schengen-style rolling windows, and why repeated entries are not the same as a clean annual allowance.

Maine's 183-day rule in plain terms: a permanent place of abode plus more than 183 days in Maine in a tax year makes you a statutory resident. How to count it, the safe harbors, and the official source.

Malta's tax residency rule in plain terms: more than 183 days in a calendar year makes you a Maltese tax resident. How to count it, what domicile and the remittance basis change, and the official source.

Maryland's 183-day rule in plain terms: a place of abode kept for more than six months plus 183 or more days in Maryland in a calendar year makes you a statutory resident. How to count it, and the official source.

Massachusetts's 183-day rule in plain terms: a permanent place of abode plus more than 183 days in Massachusetts in the taxable year makes you a statutory resident. How to count it, and the official source.

Mauritius's tax residency rule in plain terms: 183 days or more in an income year makes you a Mauritian tax resident, and a separate 270-day test looks across three years. How to count both, and the official source.
Minnesota's 183-day rule in plain terms: an abode in Minnesota plus at least 183 days in the state during the year makes you a statutory resident. How to count it, and the official source.
Nebraska's 183-day rule in plain terms: a permanent place of abode plus 183 days or more in Nebraska in a calendar year makes you a statutory resident. How to count it, and the official source.

New Jersey's 183-day rule in plain terms: a permanent home in New Jersey plus more than 183 days in the state in a calendar year makes you a statutory resident. How to count it, and the official source.
New York's 183-day rule in plain terms: a permanent place of abode plus more than 183 days in New York in a calendar year makes you a statutory resident. How to count it, and the official source.

New Zealand's 183-day tax residency rule in plain terms: more than 183 days in any 12-month period makes you a tax resident, backdated to the first of those days. How to count it, plus the permanent-place-of-abode test and the official source.
North Dakota's 7-month rule in plain terms: a permanent place of abode plus more than 210 days in North Dakota in a tax year makes you a statutory resident. How to count it, and the official source.

Ohio counts contact periods (overnights), not days. More than 212 contact periods in a calendar year makes you a presumed Ohio resident under Rev. Code §5747.24. How contact periods work, and the official source.

Oregon's 200-day rule in plain terms: maintaining a place of abode in Oregon plus more than 200 days in the state during the tax year makes you a resident, unless the stay is temporary. How to count it, and the official source.
What overstaying a visa or stay limit means, why consequences differ across immigration systems, and the key misunderstandings that put travelers at risk.

Pennsylvania's 183-day rule in plain terms: a permanent place of abode plus more than 183 days in Pennsylvania in a calendar year makes you a statutory resident. How to count it, and the official source.

Portugal's 183-day tax residency rule in plain terms: more than 183 days in any rolling 12-month period makes you a tax resident. How to count it, plus the habitual-residence test and the official source.
What proving time spent in a country is actually trying to do, how stronger and weaker evidence work together, and how to build a defensible chronology when records are incomplete.
What proving absence is actually trying to establish, which records can support it, and why a consistent chronology matters more than any one document.
What rebuilding travel history actually involves, which sources are strongest, how to handle gaps and conflicts honestly, and what makes a reconstructed timeline defensible.

Rhode Island's 183-day rule in plain terms: a permanent place of abode plus more than 183 days in Rhode Island in a calendar year makes you a resident, even if domiciled elsewhere. How to count it, and the official source.

Romania's 183-day tax residency rule in plain terms: more than 183 days in any 12 consecutive months ending in the calendar year makes you a tax resident. How to count it, plus the domicile and centre of vital interests tests and the official source.
The Schengen 90-day rule in Europe means up to 90 total days in the Schengen area within any rolling 180-day period. Plain-English explanation and examples.
Which countries count together under the Schengen 90/180 short-stay rule, and why EU membership is not the same thing as Schengen membership.
A practical walkthrough of how the Schengen 90/180 rolling window works, why days return one by one, and where manual counting goes wrong.
What a Schengen single-entry visa means: how to read the number of entries, the duration of stay, and the validity dates on a uniform (type C) visa. Once you leave, a single-entry visa is used up.

Singapore's tax residency rule in plain terms: 183 days or more of presence or employment in a calendar year makes you a Singapore tax resident. How to count it, the concessions, and the official source.
Thailand's tax residency rule in plain terms: more than 180 days in a calendar year makes you a Thai tax resident. How to count it, what it means for foreign income, and the official source.
What a travel-history record for a visa application usually needs to do, what makes it usable or weak, and where official application instructions take priority.
What a travel-history template is for, why no single template works everywhere, what fields to include, and what to verify before relying on one in a formal submission.

Turkiye's 90/180 short-stay rule in plain terms: at most 90 days within any rolling 180-day period on a visa or visa exemption. How the rolling window is counted, with a worked example and the official source.
The UAE's 183-day tax residency rule in plain terms: 183 days or more in any consecutive 12-month period makes you a UAE tax resident. How to count it, the 90-day test, and the official source.
What UK citizenship absence limits are trying to assess, how the 5-year and 3-year naturalisation residence tests are usually framed, and why timing, status history, and discretion matter.
How the UK ETA and Standard Visitor visa differ, when the distinction matters, and why neither one should be treated as guaranteed entry by itself.
What UK ILR absence limits are trying to measure, why many routes use a 180-days-in-any-12-months style test, and why route, qualifying period, and rule version matter.
What people usually mean by the UK Standard Visitor 180-day shorthand, why it is incomplete, and how visit length, travel pattern, and genuine visitor assessment fit together.
What the UK Statutory Residence Test is trying to determine, why it is not a simple 183-day rule, and how automatic tests, sufficient ties, and prior residence change the analysis.
What people usually mean by the U.S. B1/B2 180-day stay shorthand, why it is incomplete, and how visa validity, admission, and authorized stay fit together.
What the U.S. substantial presence test is trying to determine, why it is not a simple 183-day rule, and where exceptions, treaties, and professional advice matter.

The US Visa Waiver Program in plain terms: with an approved ESTA you may be admitted for up to 90 days per visit. How the limit works, why nearby-country trips don't reset it, and the official source.
Vermont's 183-day rule in plain terms: a permanent place of abode plus more than 183 days in Vermont in the taxable year makes you a statutory resident. How to count it, and the official source.

Virginia's 183-day rule in plain terms: maintaining a place of abode in Virginia for more than 183 days in the taxable year makes you an actual resident, even if domiciled elsewhere. How to count it, and the official source.
What people usually mean when they ask what counts as a day, why the answer is not universal, and where entry, exit, transit, and regime differences start to matter.
When Schengen days come back after a long stay, why they return one by one, and why there is no lump-sum reset.
